title: Quiz: Introduction to Data Encoding
description: Test your understanding of quantum data encoding methods

questions.question: How many qubits are needed to amplitude-encode a vector of 1024 values?
questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding uses log₂(N) qubits for N values. log₂(1024) = 10, so only 10 qubits are needed to encode 1024 values as amplitudes.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of angle encoding for near-term QML?

options.text: Uses fewer qubits than other methods
Simple to implement with rotation gates and scales naturally

questions.explanation: Angle encoding is practical for NISQ devices because it's easy to implement using rotation gates (Rx, Ry, Rz) and the number of qubits naturally matches the number of features.

questions.question: What is the main drawback of amplitude encoding?

options.text: General state preparation requires O(N) gates

questions.explanation: While amplitude encoding achieves exponential compression in qubits, preparing an arbitrary amplitude-encoded state generally requires O(N) gates, which can negate the space advantage.

questions.question: Basis encoding stores classical binary strings directly in computational basis states.
questions.explanation: True! Basis encoding maps a binary string b = b₁b₂...bₙ directly to the computational basis state |b₁b₂...bₙ⟩.

questions.question: In angle encoding with Ry(θ)|0⟩, what state is produced when θ = π?
questions.explanation: Ry(π)|0⟩ = cos(π/2)|0⟩ + sin(π/2)|1⟩ = 0|0⟩ + 1|1⟩ = |1⟩. A rotation of π around the Y-axis flips |0⟩ to |1⟩.

questions.question: Which encoding method is best suited for encoding a database index for quantum search?
questions.explanation: Basis encoding is ideal for encoding discrete indices because it directly maps integers to computational basis states, enabling quantum parallelism over a superposition of index values.

questions.question: Which statements about amplitude encoding are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: The encoded vector must be normalized
It provides exponential compression in qubit count
It always requires O(N) gates to prepare

questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding requires normalization (quantum states must have unit norm), provides exponential compression (log₂N qubits for N values), and can encode continuous values. It doesn't ALWAYS require O(N) gates - some special cases allow efficient preparation.
